
WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.
SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JUNE , 1883.


"FANCY'S ROAMING."

		BY L. M. HEWLINGS. 

[Affectionately inscribed to Dear Sisters and Friends
	who abide in the Beautiful Valley of Salt Lake.]

My thoughts they will go roaming
	With mixed sadness and delight--
On Fancy's wings they take me
	Over the mountain's height.
Far over the weird and fearful,
Over the strange and new, 
Till I reach the smiling valley
	Where hearts for me are true.

Fresh in its pristine beauty,
E'en as Eden seeming fair, 
With the same sweet odors laden, 
	Through all the balmy air.
Most glorious in its gleaming
	Of fruit and flower bloom,
While birds of brightest plumage
	With nature join in tune. 

Ten thousand rills are bearing
	Richest blessings as they go
Leaping down the cloud-capped mountains
	White with eternal snow.
Girdled 'round with mighty hills,
She sits a very Queen;
Polished by art--glad nature
	Has adorned with living green.

Thou peerless, charming valley!
From a desert wild brought forth;
O would that I could now recount
	The praise of toillng worth.
Could tell of those who toiled in faith,
With dauntless courage brave.
Until they found a resting place
	within the quiet grave.

waters * bitter to the taste 
Caill frosts and buring sands,
With persevering labor brought
	Fruitage from barren lands.
Where once roamed beasts and savage hordes
	O'er all the desert plain,
They tilled, and ploughed, and sowed the seed,
	That now yields golden grain.

Fierce plagues oft tried their hearts full sore 
Famine stalked through the land, 
Yet reared they huts * from out the earth
	Where now proud mansions stand. 
High places level'd, depths filled up,
Gorges and chasms dread
A mighty highway overleaps,
On which the nations tread. 

Thro' this once howling wilderness
	Of dreariness and blight,
They wrought by faith inspired by Him,
Who said, "Let there be Light."
By faith pre-visioned, oft they viewed
	This smiling  valley o'er, 
As Moses on Mount Nebo stood
	And saw fair Canaan's shore. 

They saw the solitary wastes
	Filled with abodes of men,
From north to south, from east to west,
All nations gather in. 
Cities and towns dot all the vale,
And commerce makes her way
O'er pulsing wires, with lighting speed,
Throughout the night and day. 

O wondrous change!  and passing strange
	That e'en thro' travail caome, 
This living light of beauty bright,
That covers all the plain!
I gaze enraptured on the scene
	With awe-inspired delight;
I meet and greet dear friends and true,
On this, my airy flight. 

Some dear forms lie there to rest
	Beneath the emerald sod,
While their freed spirits walk the hills
	And gardens of our God.
Softly the angels whisper,
Of things I cannot reach,
And thoughts float on in language
	Too grand to clothe in speech.  

Yet the joys of fancy's roaming,
Friends that I meet and greet,
The valley's bright adorning
	Forms a picture, passing sweet.
Atchison, Kansas, May, 1883.

* Alkaline.
* Small adobie houses.


[Column 1]
MEDITATIONS.

BY HANNAH T. KING.

	The noblest study of intelligent beings, is
the study of that complex and most wonderful
nature of which each human being is a unit;
yes, the human is a grand metaphysical nature
both in its spiritual and corporeal structure; it
is so intricate, so altogether marvelous, that we
need not go one step farther to find our Crea-
tor, our God. He has mingled His essence so
palpably in this wonderful construction that
the human and the divine cannot be separated;
the climax of this grand machine is a marvel
and a mystery, even when the anatomist lays it
bare before the eye and before the understand-
ing; all of which not even a Galen has fully
penetrated or understood; the dissecting knife
exhibits wonders—but death has then taken
possession, and has shut down the grand wheel
of life—the mainspring of earthly existence,
and the machinery stands still; its complex
and sublime working is no longer in action;
but let us imagine this beautiful, mechanical
body transparent in life! that we could watch
its matchless movements and its eternal adap-
tation for all its purposes. The life, in the
form of rich, pure blood, rushing all so noise-
lessly and methodically that the being itself is
not aware of it, rushing through that grand
citadel, the heart, (I have heard six hundred
times in one hour) and then dispersing and fill-
ing every artery and vein with renovated life
and vigor; the next grand coadjutor, the brain,
so mysterious in its affinity to the other grand
organs, surely it must contain the throne-room
where reason, judgment, thought and memory
hold their daily and hourly levee, aye, minute
by minute, and from which emanate the won-
drous tale of life, and all its grand and myste-
rious operations! And then the spirit that has
so imprest its image upon the body that has
been so wonderfully prepared for it, and
which alone sets the grand machine in motion;
that mighty spirit! how little we actually
know about it, even when it has been our co-
mate for three score years and ten, and often
more. With regard to the corporeal, scienee
opens a door, many doors and shews us much—
but after the closest research—reason tells us
we know but little, even of ourselves. How
absurd is scepticism in the face of all this ig-
norance, where the effects alone tell us there is
a mighty, but a mysterious cause, and only by
analogy can we reason out a portion of the
hidden truth. The great Creator has drawn a
line beyond which merely human intellect can
not pass. Electricity, that marvelous agent
about which so much has been discovered and

[Column 2]
proven, what we see of its effects to-day we
may cocjecture, what it will be as a powerful
agent to man even fifty years hence; yet with
all the knowledge concerning it to-day, what
this powerful and efficient agent really is the
keenest mind has not been able to discover,
though it is delightful to study and conjecture
upon it, and we know that it is a power from
the laboratory of Heaven, and ought to teach
reverence and humility even in the using of it;
the human machine is powerfully impregnated
with it—in fact all the elements are incorpora-
ted more or less in this God-like organism,
which will take eternity to fully develop in
all its compartments, yet even in this twilight
state of being, the watchful can catch glimpses
that startle and electrify the meditative; and
they realize the sublimity of the words of Holy
Writ, "We are fearfully and wonderfully
made!" I ever remember my brother's words,
and the accents in which they were uttered as
he left the Anatomical Schools in Cambridge,
he seemed absorbed in what he had just be-
held, and what had wonderfully opened and
excited his mind, "let the sceptic and the athe-
ist witness what I have just beheld, and if they
are not convinced of an Omnipotent Creator,
neither would they be persuaded though one
arose from the dead." With all these grand
mysteries and problems before our eyes, shall
we spend our lives in thoughtless frivolity, in
reckless apathy? In deathly ignorance, in
cold formality? Rather let us know ourselves
and the grandeur of our origin. Let those
who will and do glory in the patent of nobility
which they received from Westminster or St.
Paul's; we will glory that our patent was bes-
towed in the Chancery of Heaven, and en-
dorsed by the authority of the same; and let
us, as Latter-day Saints, ever bear in mind the
covenants which we have made in holy places,
and under which many of the present genera-
tion were born; let us endeavor to be wise in
our day and generation; this will detract from
no legal enjoyment, but will enhance our
pleasures and give a pungency and a potency to
them that must be realized to be understood
or appreciated; there is a laughter that makes
the ear of the listener sad and tells the phase
of character louder than words; there is a
mirth from which the wise and the good recoil;
there are palaces of gorgeous beauty, in which
the pure and the wise could not live even a
day in happiness, the sweet, humble home ever
"swept and garnished" by sweet, pure, unso-
phistocated spirits, basking in the smile and
approbation of their Heavenly Father is where
the Spirit of God visits, and inspires, sustains
and blesses, and where the hand of God is ac-
knowledged, and even His rod is kissed, for its
wounds are better than the flatteries and fri-
volities of an ignorant, supinely smiling world.
May the Spirit of the Holy One be around
His people. May they be enabled to imbibe
this Holy Esseuce, that by it they may apply
and use the noble gift bestowed upon them.
	Salt Lake City, May 24th, 1883.


The Princess Louise's training has been en-
tirely practical. She is a finished housekeeper,
and served an apprenticeship at Osborne to
cook, confectioner, laundress of fine things,
seamstress and dressmaker. Every day for
years the Queen's table at Osborne was graced
by a dish made by one of Her Majesty's daugh-
ters.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
			A VENERABLE WOMAN.
					______
		PRESENDIA LATHROP KIMBALL.
				[Continued.]
	It is difficult to write a life so varied, one
might say complex, as the one of Sister Pres-
endia satisfactorily. There are so many min-
ute details that fill in so many different places
that to make a complete whole would require
moro space than newspaper columns afford.
However, brief outlines have brought us so
far on the journey into the wilderness as to
Winter Quarters, and now let us stay awhile
in this resting place by the way and recall a
few items of interest. Here the people waited,
yes, waited for the pioneers, waited for the
Mormon Battalion, a weary waiting, a desolate
waiting.
	The Saints built log houses, "chinked in," ac-
cording to a Western phrase, and covered
generally with dirt and straw. The floors
were dirt or sometimes puncheon. When we
consider the comfortable homes the Saints had
left, the hardships and privations they had en-
dured, the hundreds of deaths by the way, we
may well say, "it was like the children of Isra-
el in the wilderness," Yet through faith they
endured without murmuring, and besought the
Lord for strength and his spirit to sustain
them. Sister Presendia's home was a very
humble one, built of logs and covered with
straw and dirt, a little straw upon the dirt
floor for cleanliness. Here she toiled day af-
ter with scanty fare, and taught the children,
and when school would close for the night her
voice would leave her with weakness and over
exertion; but she loved the children and
gained their affection.
	The people were afflicted with much sick-
ness, the water of the Missouri river was un-
healthy, it was an ague country; provisions
were scarce, and the lack of vegetables and
fruit caused scurvy and other similar com-
plaints, and on all sides there were sickness
and distress, very few escaped, and many died.
Sister Presendia was taken seriously ill the
week her school closed for the season, she was
attacked with winter fever; but she had un-
swerving faith in the ordinances of the Gospel,
and through the exercise of faith by those
around her and the careful nursing and kind
attentions of friends, she was, after a painful
and dangerous illness restored to health.
	About this time three of the brethren who
went out with the Mormon Battalion came
back to Winter Quarters, having been sent on
special business from Pueblo. One of the
three was John Tibbetts, the other names the
lady does not remember. The circumstance
made a lasting impression upon her mind, she
says, "I never beheld such a pitiful sight be-
fore as these poor, worn out travelers present-
ed. Their clothing hung in rags, their faces
burned, and with sun and snow they were
nearly blind. Their feet were wrapped in raw-
hides from the buffalo. I sat and heard them
tell how fearfully they had suffered crossing
the prairies in the dead of winter, and all this
in the employ of a government that had driven
us, defenceless women and children, into a
savage wilderness. I could not refrain from
weeping when I looked upon these my breth-
ren and realized how they had suffered."
	As early in the Spring of 1847 as the snow
left the ground, the pioneers who had been
busy preparing themselves during the winter,
left to search out a haven of refuge for the
Saints who had been compelled to flee from a
land of liberty, yes, a land of freedom, free-
dom purchased with the blood of their own
ancestors, who had themselves fled from re-
ligious persecution and tyranny. Only a few
men were left to raise grain and vegetables,
and protect the women and children. Five
hundred able-bodied men had gone to Mexico
to serve their country, and the pioneers took
away nearly one hundred and fifty more, thus
were left the aged, the feeble, the women and
the children. The sisters left almost as it
were alone, were humble and prayerful and
lived near to the Lord, otherwise they could
not have lived at all. The families of the Bat-
talion men, the families of the pioneers were
left as it were dependent upon the Lord for
protection. Sister Presendia at this time was
living with Sister Laura Pitkin, another of the
wives of Heber C. Kimball, their house was
near Sister Vilate's, and they used often to
meet and pray together, and others of the
family and neighbors would gather in. They
were much exercised in their feelings for the
pioneers who had gone out into a new and un-
discovered country, exposed to the perils of a
savage wilderness. It was a time of great
anxiety. Soon after the pioneers left, when
the weather grew warmer, another company,
consisting of all those who could fit themselves
out with teams and provisions, grain for plant-
ing and implements of husbandry, started upon
the trail of the pioneers towards the Rocky
Mountains; now indeed was the settlement al-
most deserted. The Sisters had greater need
to draw near to the Lord, and the manifesta-
tions of his goodness and power were indeed
marvelous, especially in healing the sick. The
gift of tongues and interpretation, and the gift
of prophecy were given them for their comfort
and consolation in this trying time.
---To mention one or two of the wonderful
manifestations of the healing power will surely
not be out of place. A child of Lyman Whit-
ney's (brother of Bishop N. K. Whitney) was
restored to life after it was seemingly past re-
covery. The little one had not seen or spoken
for two days, its eyeballs were dried over, the
sisters were called in to administer, Sister
Elizabeth Ann Whitney, Sister Vilate, Sister
Laura Pitkin and Presendia Kimball and one
or two others. They administered, anointing
the child with oil, and bathing its eyes with
milk and water, and it was restored to life and
health miraculously, but the sisters gave God
the glory.
	On another occasion, previously, a visit was
made at Sister Leonard's and a meeting was
held, at which Sister Eliza R. Snow, Zina D.
Young and Elizabeth Ann Whitney were
present, also Sisters Vilate and Presendia Kim-
ball and some others. Brother Leonard spoke in
tongues in an Indian language, and prophesied
of the destruction of this nation before the
coming of the Savior. The power that rested
upon him was so great as to produce such an
intense sympathy with those in the room, that
they were all wonderfully affected. Sister Eliza
Snow walked the floor to keep her breath.
All felt the distress and agony that awaited
the nation, more particularly the priests and
harlots being destroyed in their wickedness.
Sister Eliza Snow spoke afterwards in the pure
language of Adam, with great power, and the
interpretation was given.
			To be Continued.


WASTE NOT WANT NOT.

	Waste is a subject that is rarely spoken of in
connection wiih religion. "Wilful waste makes
woful want," is an old saying, also "Waste not
want not;" though the latter does not always
prove true. From Holy Writ we learn that it
is displeasing to God, and our own reason con-
firms it. It is more especially so when so many
of the earth's inhabitants fare so poorly.
	St. Peter says, "To knowledge add temper-
ance;" aud when Jesus had fed the multitude
with five loaves and two small fishes, He told
them to "Gether up the fragments that re-


mained that nothing be lost." Modern revela-
tion in the Doctrine and Covenants informs us
that God has given the beasts of the field, the
fowls of the air, the herb and the good things
that came of the earth, whether for houses,
barns, orchards or gradens, for the benefit and
use of man, to please the eye and gladden the
heart, for food and raiment, taste and smell
and to enliven the soul.
	And it pleaseth God that He hath given all
these things unto man; for unto this end were
they made, to be used with judgment, not to
excess, neither by extortion, and in nothing
doth man offend God, or against none is his
wrath kindled, save those who confess not His
hand in all things, and obey not his command-
ments.
	Vegetarianism is now attracting considerable
attention in Europe, and is advised on account
of the land being able to support three times the
people, if cattle were not fed, and the ground
planted with grain and vegetables. But God
says "Whoso forbiddeth to abstain from meat
that man should not eat the same is not or-
dained of God;" but "Wo be unto that man
that sheddeth blood, or that wasteth flesh and
hath no need." Doc. and Cov. It is also given
that it is pleasing to Him that meat should
only be eaten in the time of winter, or excess
of hunger, and all things are to be used with
judgment and not to excess.
	Also cruelty to animals, though not often
considered religiously, is not the less worthy of
our condemnation, while their general inability
to defend themselves should implore our clem-
ency. It is said that much torture is inflicted
on animals by physiologists and that physiology
cannot posssbly progress except by means of
experiments on living animals. A humane
writer says "You may buy knowledge at the
cost of sin, and often do so in scientific investi-
gations.
	We do not remember reading any scripture
on the subject, but it seems reasonable that
God does not like his dumb creatures to suffer,
especially as it is written that a sparrow does
not fall to the ground without His notice.
Christ's commandment to "Do unto others as
ye would that they should do unto you" is
probably not out of place in regard to the
brute creation, and certianly to torment and
abuse them is the reverse of mercy; kindness
and compassion, which attributes we are en-
couraged to cultivate and practice.
	ANN FELLOWS.


MISCELLANEOUS.

	We should do everything we can for others,
if only to dissipate the thought of what they
omit to do for us.—Madame Swetchine.
	"The time has come when scientific truth
must cease to be the property of a few. It
must be woven into the common life of the
world."—Agassiz.
	Habit, if wisely and skillfully formed, be-
comes truly a second nature, as the common
saying is; but unskilfully and unmethodically
directed, it will be as it were the ape of nature,
which imitates nothing to the life, but only 
clumsily and awkwardsly.—Bacon.
	We want one man to be always thinking and
another to be always working, and we call one
a gentleman and the other an operator; where-
as, the workman ought often to be thinking and
the thinker often to be working, and both
should be gentlemen in the best sense. The
mass of society is made up of morbid thinkers
and miserable workers. It is only by labor
that thought can be made healthy, and only by
that thought can be made happy, and
the two cannot be separated with impunity.—
Ruskin.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 3


MAY.

ADDRESSED TO BISHOP O. F. WHITNEY.

Bright harbinger of Summer, scented May,
	Spring's fairest month, thrice welcome thou,
For thee the mavis tunes her sweetest lay,
	The linnet warbles on the hawthorn bough.
List, list, what music greets my ear,
	What sound reverberates through the dell,
Is it the crow of the chanticleer,
	Or matin chime of village bell?
Wake, wake, my harp, and join the song,
	And thou, my lute, assist the lay,
For Pheobus drives his steeds along
	To herald in the joyous May.
Bright Sol illumes the mountain crest,
	And morn her wings outspreads,
The swallow peers from out her nest
	Beneath the sheltering leads.
The dew from off the skylark's wing
	Like diamond sparks are thrown,
The rising makes the welkin ring
	With music all her own.
The buttercup with golden crest,
	The modest primrose pale,
And violet in azure drest
	Bedecks the fragrant vale,
And like the daisy, greets the day,
	Who wide her petals spreads,
As conscious of the breath of May
	They rear their perfumed heads.
Gay Flora's bud crowned nymphs are seen
	To scatter flowers with lavish hand.
Pomona come in mantle green
	To strew her fruit bloom o'er the land.
Gay Momus dons his cap and bells,
	And youth is decked in ribbons gay,
For mirth, and joy, and pleasure tells
	That this is blitbesome, smiling May.
Haste, haste, my friends, and join the throng
	For Pan has tuned his pastoral reed,
Terpsichore leads the dance along
	And trips it lightly o'er the mead.
Behold, 'tis Venus' chariot comes
	Drawn by a pair of turtle doves:
She pays a visit to our homes
	With all her train of dimpled loves.
And see, from out yon busy town,
	The boisterous crowd come rushin on,
The sternest face has lost its frown
	And bade dull care awhile begone:
The pole is hnng with garlands sweet,
	For lasses choose their queen to-day,
While youth and age together meet
	To celebrate the first of May.
						MARTHA A. GAULTON.


[Column 1]

INFLUENCES AND DISCIPLINE.

	How true it is that outward objects and sur-
roundings affect us in this life. There are—
there must be certain unseen influences too
that exalt or debase, as well as that which we
see and hear. Place a child in an atmosphere
of wickedness, where he hears bad language, and
is a witness to dishonesty, duplicity and mean-
ness, and it becomes second nature to him al-
most invariably, even though he may have
been born of goodly parents and with unvitia-
ted blood. And vice versa, though there may
be exeptions to this general rule. Tempta-
tions are incidental to human life, there is no
doubt but this is strictly in accordance with
certain laws and principles marked out as pre-
paratory, or educational to obtaining a higher
state of perfection. Trials, we are told, purify
us as gold seven times tried. If we possess
pure gold, or its equivalent, in our souls we
shall never know it unless we pass through the
trying and purifying process. And yet our
natural inclinations would lead us to avoid all
that would be calculated to stir our hearts to
their very depths, and open up the fountains
of sorrow, or perhaps bitterness. But fortu-
nately, it may be for us, we have not exactly
the, shaping of our own destiny, though we
may cut and carve away at it considerably,
and sometimes mar it, in attempting to mold it

[Column 2]

to our ideas. Life is a beautiful thing after
all, notwithstanding all the hard knocks and
bruises one gets, and the rough paths our feet
must sometimes tread on in its great highway.
The jostling, and crowding, and pushing
among our fellow men makes us more
mindful of others, finding we must give a
little, yield a point, and that others have
rights that must be respected as well as our-
selves. Otherwise we might be intensely sel-
fish, as some are inclined to be even now,
though we get so much discipline by contact
with the great world of mankind. But, as I
said at first, people certainly partake of the
scenes among which they live. This is a fact
that cannot be denied. If oceans, mountains,
lakes and rivers, prairies, valleys and forests
effect in a large degree the stronger and more
prominent traits of character, then why not in
the finer qualities of heart and mind by the
beautiful in art, in music, in poetry and the re-
finement in the homes of the people, be they
rich or poor, effect them by association even
though they may not receive a high degree of
cultivation in any one of these branches. To
live among refined people is in itself cultiva-
tion; to live among beautiful objects, if one
has the sense to appreciate, is impressional; to
hear good music is to be emotional; there is
nothing more powerfully emotional than music;
even the music of the waterfall, the murmur-
ing of a river as it winds its way towards the
ocean, but more grand and sublime still, the
murmuring sea, music indeed, music that lives
forever afterwards in the soul. One who has
lived in early life, or for many years by the
seashore, can never forget the delightful sound
of the waves as they dash and break against
the beach, or in soft murmurs lull one to sleep
in their gentler and more placid moods. It
comes back again ever and anon, fresh and
natural in after years like a sweet melody.
Music is, and must be, the highest human
sense, the nearest approach to Deity. The
Creator has all things set to music, and in the
most delicate rhythm, and we only catch here
and there a whisper, an undertone of the di-
vine inspiration. It is so far above our poor
comprehension, yet by and bye we may under-
stand. Poetry is one of the elements that con-
stitute higher attributes, and to properly ap-
preciate true poetry one must be possessed of a
high order of intellect, must have some con-
ception of music, and be intensely inspiration-
al. I am aware that many will dispute this,
nevertheless I maintain the theory. Who can
judge poetry but a poet, one whose whole soul
revels in the undiscovered realms of that fair
Elysium that lifts you into another atmos-
phere than that of this mundane sphere. Who
can criticise music but a musician whose very
life is not only devoted to it, but enraptured in
its expression? Who should judge the merits
of a painting but an artist, and one who is
able to grasp the subject in its idealistic sense
as well as in the actual developement worked
out by the production upon the canvas. Yet
one may be greatly influenced by any and all
of these things without fully appreciating
them. And indeed much happiness of a fine
quality is given from these sources. It is ele-
vating to live among beautiful scenes out and
in doors, for they beautify and give impres-
sions to our lives that are lasting in character.
They tend to gentler ways and softer morals,
make us more charitable and forgiving, more
in sympathy. with the better side of humanity,
and more lenient towards those who lack in
grace and excellence of attainment. No miser
ever lives among beautiful scenes. Their walls
are bare, their clothing scanty, their lives bar-
ren of good deeds. The remembrance of beau-
tiful scenes, beautiful things and their associa-
tion clings to us as long we live, even though
great changes may transpire in our circum-
stances.

[Column 3]

"Long, long be my heart with such memories filled,
Like the vase in which roses have once been distilled,
You may break, you may ruin the vase if you will,
But the scent of the roses will hang round it still."

Therefore let us gather around us as much of
the beautiful as we can, not encroaching upon
the privileges of others, and when we have that
which is pleasant and happifying let us give
God the glory, and let our hearts swell with
gratitude to Him that "our lives are cast in
pleasant places." And let no one be selfish in
appropriating the gifts of heaven and shutting
out their brethren and sisters who are not so
highly favored, but enlarge their hearts in
proportion to the bounties heaven has bes-
towed, and dispense with a liberal hand to
those in need. Thus may the blessings of hea-
ven descend, for the poor are the Father's es-
pecial care, "for theirs is the kingdom of hea-
ven." And it is true that we can see the ne-
cessity of discipline. The Lord's ways are not
as our ways, and we do not know the ways and
means He makes use of to school our spirits
and bring us into nearness to Him.
	Spiritual impressions are similar in their bear-
ings to material ones; one sees a vision, the
Spirit of the Lord may even accompany it, yet
the whole force of its meaning and influence
may not come at once with all its thrilling
emotions, the soul-absorbing sensations that
follow and linger in the brain, the mind, and
the heart, and often influence one's whole life
afterwards. One who possesses spiritual powers
cultivated to a high extent is always companion-
able, though the education in other respects may
have been neglected.	AUNT EM.


﻿EDITORIAL NOTES.
                        ______
     In our last number, May 15th, a poem by
Lula was published, entitled “Blessings," and
addressed to Sister Presendia L. Kimball, but
by mistake the name of the lady to whom it
was addressed was omitted, which we sincerely
regretted.	
     On Monday, May 11th, Sister E. R. S. Smith
left the city to attend the Woman’s Conference
in Brigham City and travel through several set-
tlements in that Stake of Zion, to visit Washa-
kee among other places and assist the Elders
in the organization of a Relief Society there
among the Lamanites. She has been absent
ever since, traveling most of the time, and
speaking to the people.
    Mrs. H. H. Jackson, a lady well and favora-
bly known to the public as an anthor and con-
tributor to the Century Magazine and Atlan-
tic Monthly, has been spending a day or two
in the city. We have had the privilege of
some pleasant talks with her and feel greatly
pleased with her acquaintance. Once known
she could never be forgotten. She is making
notes of some of the distinctive features of
our belief and history as a people, and will
certainly do justice to the subject from her
stand point.
    On Wednesday, May 23rd, the Annual
meeting of the Primary Association of South
Cottonwood was held, over one hundred child-
ren present, about fifty boys ranging from five
to about fourteen yearn old. The exercises
were well rendered. Mrs. Elizabeth Davis
presiding, assisted by her Counselors, Mrs.
Margaret Cahoon and Mrs. Wooton.  The
yearly report was an excellent one, and the
prospects are good for still greater improve-
ment in the future. The meeting was ad-
dressed by E. C. Clawson, E. B. Wells, Louie
Felt, Mary A. Freeze and other ladies.


Be not simply good—be good for something.
—Thoreau.

SALT LAKE CITY.	-	JUNE 1, 1883.


ANNUAL MEETING OF THE D. H. A.

	The first Annual Meeting of the Deseret Hos-
pital Association was held in the Council House,
in this city, Saturday, May 12th ult., Mrs. Eliza
R. Snow Smith presiding. After singing the
hymn beginning "Let Zion in her beauty rise,"
prayer was offered by Mrs. Sarah M. Kimball,
then singing again, "O, my Father, thou that
dwellest," after which the following report of
the secretary for the past year was submitted:
	One year ago to-day a meeting was held in
this house, to consider the matter of organizing
an Association, with a view to the establishment
of a hospital. About twenty-five ladies were
present, and nominations of officers for the as-
sociation were made, and a decision arrived at
to pay an initiatory fee of one dollar each as a
condition of membership, and to elect by vote a
President, Vice-President, Executive Board,
Secretary and Treasurer. Accordingly the fol-
lowing ladies were elected: President, Eliza R.
Snow Smith; Vice-President, Zina D. H. Young;
N. Hyde, Phoebe Woodruff, Bathsheba M.
Smith and Jane S. Richards; Secretary, E. B.
Wells; Treasurer, M. M. Barrat; as an Honor-
ary Committee, all the Stake Presidents of Re-
lief Societies; Dr. E. B. Ferguson was engaged
as Resident Surgeon; Dr. R. B. Pratt was chosen
Visiting  Surgeons; Mary Emma Vanschooven,
clinical registrar and dispensary clerk; and
Mary Ann McLean, Matron; Visiting Commit-
tees and a Finance Committee were subsequent-
ly chosen. At a meeting held on the 15th.
of May, it was decided to rent a building until
some better arangement could be made, and
committees were appointed to canvass the sever-
al parts of the city, and adjacent districts, and
see what means could be obtained as donations
towards defraying the expenses of establishing
such an institution. A petition was drawn up
and submitted to President Taylor for approval,
and the canvassing committees began their
work. These ladies were enegetic and success-
ful, and meetings were held twice and even
three and four times a week, and reports were
made from the several committees, who had
the labor in hand. President Taylor was noti-
fied from time to time of the proceedings of the
committees, and gave advice in regard to the
movement.
	A circular was issued throughout the various
Stakes of Zion, giving the names of the officers
 of the institution and regulations made to enter
oractically upon the work. One of these was
the suggestion that each Belief Society and Y.
L. and Y. M. M. I. Association should pay
statedly one dollar per month as a regular sub-
scription to assist in defraying the current ex-
penses of the hospital, and that the amount
paid stand as a credit in favor of any member of
the respective Society or Association that should
require the benefit of the same.
	Finally a building considered suitable for the
purpose of a hospital was rented in the Twelfth
Ward, in this city, at $60 per month, and prep-
arations made to formally open the hospital.
A committee waited upon President Taylor,
and invited him to be present at the dedication,
with others of the leading brethren of the
Church, on Monday, July 17th, at ten o'clock a.
m., and at that time the Firsst Presidency of the
Church were all present, also Apostle F. D.
Richards and other brethren, and of the presi-
dency of this Stake of Zion, Elder Joseph E.
Taylor. After the dedicatory services the Resi-
dent Surgeon and Matron were set apart by the
brethren. Patients were received at the hospital,
and business transacted in the name of the
Deseret Hospital Association.
	Some necessary changed have been made since
the organization, from time to time, two ladies
have been added to the Executive Board; Dr.
Romania B. Pratt and Dr. Ellis R. Ship and Dr.
E. S. Barney, to the Visiting Surgeons; the Res-
ident Surgeon resigned at the regular monthly
meeting, Oct. 2nd, and Dr. M. P. Hughes was
engaged to fill the vacancy. In November it
was considered wise to dispense with the ser-
vices of the Dispensary Clerk, in order to econo-
mize. In March the Matron gave notice of her
desire to resign, and at the meeting in April it
was decided to engage Mrs. Harriet Ellis in her
place. Mrs. Ellis was installed as Matron on
the first of May, and is now practically filling
the position.
	The people have donated liberally to the hos-
pital, but the officers of the Association find
that with rent to pay, and necessary help to
keep up the institution, the enterprise requires
strict economy and active executive ability. The
ladies of the Executive Board, who manage the
benevolent institution have certainly shown
great zeal and earnestness in this philanthropical
work, and been determined to make the effort a
successful one for the benefit of the sick and
afflicted among the Latter-day Saints. Through
good and evil report they have been untiring in
diligence and good works, and still feel deter-
mined to persevere in well doing. Only those
who have practically labored in this direction
can appreciate the immense effort that has been
made, and the arduous labors undertaken to ac-
complish what has already been done in this di-
rection, with the sole motive of ameliorating the
condition of suffering humanity. At the pres-
ent time the Association has enrolled eight-hun-
dred and forty-five members, who are expected
to pay an annual fee of one dollar per year.
	Financial report. Total cash received during
the year, $4,772.65; orders, $1,092.05; total, $5,-
864.70. There have been other donations of
lumber, flour, meat, cow and calf, produce and
other things; quilts, blankets, bed linen, etc.
Most of THESE articles have been given by the
Relief Society and Y. L. M. I. A. in the several
Stakes of Zion. Expended in cash for fitting up
and furnishing the building as well as current
expenses since opening the Hospital, July 17,
1882, $3,705.28; Total amount of expense in or-
ders, etc., $1,044.55; Total expense, $4,749.83; Re-
maining in cash, $1,067.37; Orders, 47.50; Total,
$1,114.87; The fees from patients received during
the year amount to $754.75 cash; Other things,
$337.30; making in all, $1,092.05.
	After the report had been read and approved
the President made a few remarks, reviewing
briefly some of the labors of the past year and
expressing her gratitude to all who had aided in
the work in any way. She also expressed her
gratitude to President Taylor for his wise counsel,

[Column 3]
and excellent judgment which had been of
great benefit to her, and those associated with
her in the labor. Referred gratefully to the ef-
ficient help and aid received from the Bishop of
the ward, and thanked all who had been helpful
in any way, for it had certainly been a great
blessing to comfort and benefit the sick and
aflicted, and to think that our people can have a
place of their own, where they could receive
medical and surgical assistance, and the admin-
istration of the ordinances of the Gospel where
faith could be exercised for them. The Presi-
dent said the Unity Club had made great efforts
to help the institution and contributed liberally,
indeed they stood foremost in that respect, i. e.,
aiding the hospital. There had been many other
large and small donations.
	Sister M. Isabella Horne made a few remarks,
and added, Sister Eliza expressed her feelings on
the subject. She realized that many of the
patients had received great blessings from the
Lord through fasting and the laying on of hands.
	Dr. Elvira S. Barney stated to the meeting
the proposition she had made to the sisters to
teach a class in obstetrics, anatomy and physi-
ology. It was to the effect that she would give
the teaching free, and only receive bare remun-
eration for room and other services rendered.
The students must find their own provisions,
but a cook would be furnished to prepare the 
meals, also to do the washing for the class.
Sister Barney made other explanations in detail.
	Sister Jane S. Richards of Ogden said she was
pleased with Dr. Barney's proposition, thought
it a very liberal one, thought it essential we
should have more efficient nurses and compet-
ent accouchers through the country settle-
ments, referred to her living at a distance from
the hospital, which made it more inconvenient
for her to attend the meetings of the Executive
Board than the other ladies, but had always
made it a point to come when it was possible,
and also to assist all in her power in every de-
partment of the labor to benefit the institutione
	Dr. Romania B. Pratt said that as far as she
was acquainted with the practical workings of
the hospital it was managed in the best interests
of the association and doing great good. That
the physicians connected with it acted upon the
hightest principles of skill and science, their
works assisted by faith. Being a young institu-
tion there were many disadvantages under
which those interested had labored.
	The motion was made to change the name of
the Finance Committee to Ways and Means
Committee, which was carried unanimously.
The Committee was authorized to act in behalf
of the association, in making arrangements for
the concerts to be given in the Tabernacle, on
the 24th of July, and the Chairman of the Com-
mittee was authorized to call to her aid such
other help as she needed for the work. The
several Canvassing Committees were honorably
discharged from further duty. After some re-
marks from two or three of the Ways and Means
Committee in reference to their special depart-
ment the meeting was adjourned for one year.


EDITORIAL NOTES. 

	A good place for invalids is to go to the Elec-
tric Baths at Mrs. Dr. Hereford's, Thirteenth
Ward, on Third South Street, between First and
Second East Streets. The baths are medicated
as well as electric, and are very exhilarating.
They are specially good in cases where there is
a lack of circulation of the blood, or any indica-
tions of paralysis, also for nervous complaints.
Mrs Horsford is a very genial, affable lady, and
takes great pains to make everything pleasant
and easy. There is a bed to lie down upon in
the room after having taken the bath, which Is
quite a consideration for delicate people.


[Column 1]

	WE had the pleasure of meeting several ladids
and gentlemen of the Boston Excursion party,
among them Mr. and Mrs. Goddard, Major and
Mrs. Pierce from Orange, Mass., who live near
our own home, and who were well acquainted
with some of our relatives, which was especially
gratifying. They expressed themselves pleased
with our city, and particularly with the sermon
of President George Q. Cannon, delivered on
Sunday in the large Tabernacle.

	THE ladies will remember doubtless that this
number—June 1st—commences Volume 12 of
the EXPONENT. It is expected that we shall
have a cover for the paper soon, and indeed had
anticipated commencing this volume with it, but
unavoidable delays have made it impossible to
get the necessary forms here in time, and we do
not think it wise to wait, but pos-ibly it will be
ready by the 1st of July. It will give the paper
a better appearance for a lady's parlor or draw-
ing-room table. We trust the friends and pa-
trons of the "Mormon" woman's paper will re-
new their subscriptions and thereby help to
made it popular, as well as a success financially.
It is already much sought after by strangers and
tourists as a method of finding out something
of the women of this peculiar people who are so
much spoken against all over the world.

	ON pioneer day, the 24th of July, the memor-
able anniversary of the entrance of the pioneers
into Salt Lake Valley, there will be two grand
concerts given in the large Tabernacle in the
city, under the musical direction of Brother
Evan Stephens, the chlidren's favorite. These
two concerts are for the benefit of the Sunday
School Union, the Deseret Hospital and Brother
Stephens' classes. The proceeds to be equally
divided between the three institutions after all
expenses have been paid. Two preliminary
meetings have already been held and the pre-
paratory work commenced. Brother Stephens
will bring several hundred children from Ogden
and Logan, who have been and are now being
trained under his care, to unite with his classes
here in the city in the grand choruses. Twelve
hundred little singers in the Tabernacle will be
a wonderful attraction. The programme is not
yet quite completed, but will be issued very
soon in pamphlet form and scattered throughout
the several settlements north, south, east and
west. It will be a mammoth affair, and in con-
sideration of the day and object in view, it ought
to bring thousands of people from other places.
The officers of the Sunday School Union act as a
committee in behalf of that institution, Brother
George Goddard, Assistant Supt. of Sunday
Schools, Chairman; and "Ways of Means
Committee" of the Deseret Hospital, Mrs. Sarah
M. Kimball, Chairman, are acting in behalf of
the Association; these Commitiees will call to
their aid such other help as they require in order
to make the affair a complete success. Two
special committees have already been appointed.
Elder George Goddard, Mrs. Annie G. Sharp
and Mrs Priscilla Jennings, Committee to make
arrangements with the railroads for special rates
etc., and Mrs. E. B. Wells, Elder Abram H-
Cannon and Brother Evan Stephens, Committee
on programme. The display will be a magnifi-
cent one, and will be worth seeing.
	The concert will open with music on the great
organ by Prof. Joseph Daynes; then comes
America's grand national anthem "Hail Col-
umbia by a grand chorus of twelve hundred
voices, orchestra and organ accompaniments
with twelve hundred American flags waving.
This followed by other national American songs,
chorus by the full company. The "Ho for
Europe," England comes first, a youth bears
the banner with the British colors, and "Rule
Brittania" resounds from the orchestra, a gentle-

[Column 2]
man dressed in the British uniform sings the
grand old song, while up goes twelve hundred
fans with England's colors Scotland comes
next, with the uational airs and bagpipes, a boy
in costume bearing the banner with the thistle,
and the songs of the country are sung. Next
the Welch banner is borne to the front, and the
favorite songs of "Wild Wales." The full chorus
take up the refrain in English. Then a grand
chorus in Welch. Now to the Emerald Isle, and
the banner with the Irish shamrock song, "The
Wearing of the green," and the fans are changed
to green—then for the "Lily of LaBelle France,
and the Marseille, changing the color again
which will with other items conclude the first
part of the programme of the first concert, be-
ginging again with Germany, costume etc., and
the old song "Watch on the Rhine," and still
another change of colors. Switzerland and Italy
follow, and then northward to Scandinavia,
of which Utah contains a very large representa-
tion, and these different countries will have fair
and excellent representation, in their favorite
music, songs and costumes, with banners, mot-
toes, etc.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


BELLE HARRIS.

	I have great pleasure in answering the oft re-
peated question: "Who is Belle Harris?" Belle
Harris is a daughter of Charles Harris, son of
the late Emer Harris, brother to Martin Harris,
one of the three witnesses to the Book of Mor-
mon. Her parents are early and highly res-
pected, citizens of Utah. The character of the
family is beyond suspicion for virtue and integ-
rity, and devoted to their religion as Latter-day
Saints. I believe they were all born in the
Church. Belle is and always was a firm believer in
the testimony of her great Uncle Martin Harris,
and is devoted to all the doctrines of the Church
of which she has always been a highly esteemed
and respected member. She is intelligent and
industrious.
	The present unhallowed persecution is the
only intimation I ever heard of her reputation
being questioned. As to her marriage relations
I need not inquire, nor would it reflect any credit
to me as a lawyer to entertain the remotest
doubt as to whether her answer to the question
as to whether she was married or single was a
privileged one. It matters not whether she is
viewed as a plural wife, prostitute, single wife,
or no wife. I have already stated that her
character is beyond reproach and that point must
not be forgotten.
	Belle Harris stands upon a higher plane, and
in keeping with her higher nature, and as any
virtuous lady should do, says to the jury and the
Court: "Gentlemen, you have no legal right to
ask this question and I decline to answer it."
The question is an insult and a vile insinuation
of departed virtue; and yet were she a public
prostitute no such question, either single or
conglomerated as hers was would ever be asked.
Who ever knew in a single court of free Amer-
ica, such a question to be put to a frmale who
was dragged into court without her consent?
Echo answers, "who?" The court records may
be searched in vain, except in some of the fanat-
ical courts of Utah.
	I admit that in case an unmarried woman has
illegitimate issue and claims support from her
seducer, such a question would be legally proper
and would be asked by HER attorney, as a neces-
sary foundation to the establishment of the
plantiff's claim, but who ever knew of a Court
dragging such a woman before him and com-
pelling her to convict even her paramour. Surely
consistency is a jewel.
	The object of this communication is not to
question the sincerity of Judge Twiss. I am


not yet converted to the belief that he is design-
edly wrong, notwithstanding our wide legal dif-
ference on this point, but must set this action
down as one of those errors he so sincerely and
often has stated he is liable to.
	DANIEL TYLER.
May 19th, 1883.


IN MEMORIAM.

	It is with feelings of the deepest sorrow and loving
remembrance that we are called upon to record the
death of Sister Alice Warburton Needham, which took
place at her residence in the Seventh Ward, May l0th,
1883.
	Deceased was born at Darsbury, Cheshire, England,
March 10th, 1826. She was married to her husband,
Brother James Needham, in 1850, and immediately af-
terwards emigrated to America, and settled in Kanes-
ville, (now Council Bluffs) Iowa, in which place she re-
ceived the Gospel and was baptized. She went to St.
Louis in 1852, where she resided until 1854, when she
came to Utah with her husband. In 1855 she came to
reside in the Seventh Ward, and continued to reside
up to the time of her death. She became a member of
the Relief Society of the Ward at the time of its re-or-
ganization, and filled successively the office of Coun-
selor and President, to which latter office she was cho-
sen May 20th, 1872, and continued to hold the same un-
til her death.
	Sister Needham was a faithful wife, a true and
affectionate mother and a fatihful friend. From the
time that she embraced the Gospel she never faltered in
her faith, and her whole life was devoted to the dis-
charge of her duties as a Latter-day Saint in every po-
sition in which she was placed. She leaves a husband,
three children, seven grandchildren and a host of loving
relatives and friends to mourn her loss.
	The follwoing resolutions of respect to our beloved
President, Sister Alice W. Needham, who departed this
life May 10th, 1883, in the fifty-seventh year of her age,
were unanimously adopted by the Relief Society of the
Seventh Ward of Salt Lake City:
	Whereas, God, in his infinite wisdom, has called from
our midst our President, who was greatly beloved and
respected by us all for her kind and gentle disposition,
her pure, virtuous and exemplary life, her faithful
labors in our Society, and the many good qualities
she possessed.
	Therefore, be it resolved that we, the members of the
Relief Society of the Seventh Ward of Salt Lake City,
do deeply mourn the loss of our beloved President,
Sister Alice W. Needham, and that we condole with her
husband, children and relatives in their great loss. But
while mingling our tears with theirs, we would point
with joy to the hope of a glorious ressurrection which
all true Latter-day Saints possess.
	And be it resolved, that we present a copy of these
resolutions to her husband, and that a copy be sent to
the WOMAN'S EXPONENT for publication, and that they
be spread upon our record book, that the knowledge of
her virtue and inestimable goodness may be handed
down to future generations for their guidance and emu-
lation.
			HARRIET WOODBURY,
[Signed]		ELLEN DINWOODEY,		Counselors.
				MARY ALICE LAMBERT,
					Secretary.

real and true generosity; he can never go be-
yond mere beneovlence. --Talleyrand
  Louisa B. Stephens is the first woman to be-
come President of a bank. She succeeds her
husband in the First National Bank of Marion, 
Iowa.
  Life is made up, not of great sacrifices or 
duties, but of little things, in which smiles and 
kindnesses and small obligations, given habitu-
ally, are what win and preserve the heart, and 
secure comfort-- Sir H. Davy.
   Maria Mitchel, Professor of Astronomy in
Vassar College, has received the degree of L. L
D. from Hannover College, Madison, Ind., the 
first time this degree has been conferred on a 
woman.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


SCENES IN NAUVOO, AND INCIDENTS
	FROM H. C. KIMBALL’S JOURNAL.
[line break]
BY HELEN MAR WHITNEY.
[line break]
	Father makes frequent mention of gather-
ings at the Mansion and other places, in which
we took part, also of entertainments given at
the Concert Hall, and of presiding there a
number of times, which has served to refresh
my memory. Several familiar items I found
written in a small pocket diary which had been
overlooked. The following he wrote: "Febru-
ary 1st, 1845.	*	*	At. ten o’clock in
the evening Elder B. Young and myself went
to see Joseph and Hyrum.” I remembered this
incident and that he brought home a small
scrap from each of their burial robes. Their
bodies had been kept hidden till this time upon
Joseph’s premises and closely guarded, as there
had been large sums of money offered for them
by different parties to speculate upon; but the
Saints were never so poor nor so destitute
of human feelings as to commit such an act
towards any one, much less men so beloved
and revered as were the Prophet Joseph and
his brother Hyrum.
	Father speaks of frequent visits paid to
their mother and their to widows, Emma and
Mary Smith. The 25th of April following, he
mentions "assembling at the Mansion in com-
pany with five of the Twelve, Sister Emma
and Dr. Bernhisal to see the giants, Frederic
W. Randall and his lady. He afterwards
took me with other members of his family to
see them. None others were present but Brig-
ham Young, which circumstance was impressed
upon my mind by his looking so diminutive as
they stood side by side with the giant. He
wore an immense gold watch and chain, also a
large pin and finger ring, the latter, which we
handled, was so large that it went easily over
three of the President’s largest fingers. He
was heavily built and had a deep and power-
ful voice. His wife was about the right hight
for him, but did not seem to enjoy going about
the world for a show as much as he did, being
in a delicate state of health, she expressed her
repugnance to this kind of life and a desire for
a more quiet one back in their native land
among the highlands.
	They reminded us so of the characters we
read of in the “Scottish chiefs" and other
novels of the olden times, when their brave
chieftians and ladies of high degree were all
large in stature. The Book of Mormon and
Bible also, tell us that there were “giants in
those days."
	On the “6th of February,” father wrote,
“Last night I dreamed I was at work on my
wheel, with others, in the pottery and putting
vessels in the kiln for burning. Several were
assisting—the work was great." He also men-
tions going with my mother to a potter shop,
and says, “I made a crock upon the wheel—
the first one for nine years.” Those acquainted
with him knew him to be a potter, this was his
favorite trade, and he frequently made use of
the parable as Jesus did. He first learned the
blacksmith trade, when but a boy in the State
of Vermont, which business he carried on in
the winter and the pottery during the warm
weather. Both were very profitable trades
and would have made him a wealthy man, but
instead he willingly gave his time and tal-
ents towards the great work of building up our
Father’s Kingdom.
	He gives the following account of their,
April Conference in 1845, which was held, after
the first day, in one of the valleys a little south
of the Temple, the weather being very windy
and dusty:
	"April 6th. The Saints convened at ten
o’clock at the stand, being fifteen years since
the organization of the Church of Jesus Christ

[second column]

of Latter-day Saints, and there are one-third
more people now than there ever were before
at any conference since the Church began.	*
I called the meeting to order and offered
prayer.	B. Young preached on the subject of
baptism for the dead.	*	*	*	We met
at two o’clock—two acres were covered as
thick as they could stand—four on a square
yard—reckoned to be twenty thousand Saints.
Meeting opened by O. Hyde; then the little
children were blessed. We came to a close at
jive in the afternoon. The wind blew hard
and very dusty."
	“Monday, 7th. Met at ten o’clock in a val-
ley among the hills. Spent the forenoon talk-
ing about the Temple and Nauvoo House. In
the afternoon the revelation on the Nauvoo
House and tithing was read by Elder O. Pratt,
then Elder B. Young spoke.” 	*	*	*
"April 8th.	*	*	I spoke one hour in
the morning—Elder B. Young filled up the
time.	The afternoon was occupied by B.
Young and O. Hyde. At five o’clock the con-
ference adjourned till next October. All went
off well.”
	On the evening of the 9th, he mentions be-
ing at the Concert Hall and enjoying the beau-
tiful singing and music, etc. At a meeting
held the next evening he speaks of G. J.
Adams and Samuel Branson being cut off
from the Church for bad conduct, also about
getting the Masonic Hall for a printing office.
“This lodge,” he says, “was organized on the
15th day of March, 1842, with forty members.
Joseph Smith was made a Mason on the same
evening, Abraham Jones was present and
acted as Master. The first night he (Joseph)
took the first and second degree, the next
night took the third degree.” The following I
copy from his largest journal:
	“Joseph and Hyrum Smith were Master
Masons, yet they were massacred through the
instrumentality of some of the leading men of
hat fraternity through the States.	*	*
Although bound under the strongest obliga-
tions to be true and faithful to each other in
every case and under every circumstance, the
commission of crime excepted.”
	There is no denying that there were some
mean and disreputable characters among us,
the same as are found in all other commnni-
ties, and those who (Judas like) sought to
wind themselves into the good graces of both
parties; and according to Jesus’ words the net
was to gather of all kinds; but those who have
not already met their just rewards, will in due
time, let him be “Mormon,” Gentile or Jew,
or whatever name they may be called by, each
one will be made to answer for his or her own
sins, whatever crime they may have committed
it will return four-fold upon their own heads.
	It seems it was the 12th of the following
month that my father and several of his breth-
ren found it necessary to conceal themselves to
escape the clutches of their enemies who came
there to serve their writs. Father, B. Young
and W. Richards went to the house of Bishop
Edward Hunter that day with the Church
History and hid, in the same upper room where
Joseph had previously been concealed. W.
Richards was then sick with the chills and
fever, and President Young’s health was also
quite poor. Father wrote the morning follow-
ing, "Had a good night’s rest, B. Young
dreamed of seeing a vessel blown on to dry
land—I dreamt of saying some persons from
being drowned.	*	*	At nine o’clock G.
A. Smith came to us.	*	*	 Read history
all day, taking turns. When it came night B.
Young and I went home.	Found my family
well, but lonesome.	Returned the same night
to onr lodging.”	14th he wrote, “Elder Rich-
ards better, but B. Young quite poorly; the
day wet and chilly.	*	*	*	Wrote a
line to my wife, who came and took dinner
with us, had a good visit.	*	*	"15th.

[third column]

Last night I dreamed I saw many serpents--
they jumped at me, but‘did not bite.” At four
o’clock in the morning Joseph Kingsbury took
him in his buggy to Aaron Farr’s.
	This place was a mile or two from the city,
on the edge of a thick wood, where he staid
that day. My mother went in the afternoon
with Sanih Kingsbury to see him. He wrote
that he went into the forest twice to pray the
second time accompanied by my mother. They
left in the edge of the evening—he to meet
with the Holy Order at W. Richards’—from
there he came home, then went back to Bishop
Hunter’s."
	On the; 19th, he says,	*	*	Vilate with
Sarah Ann Kingsbury took me in a buggy to
Brother Robert Pierce’s, where I found Brig-
ham Young, John Taylor and George A. Smith.
We rested all day and felt refreshed—received
great kindness from sister Pierce and her two
daughters, Mary and Margerete; may the
Lord bless them with peace and plenty. After
dark went through the city disguised—saw
many of the Saints, but they knew me not;
went home where I found my wife Vilate, and
all well but lonesome—went back to my lodg-
ing at eleven—almost as light as day.”
	Next day he wrote that Willard Richards’
came there with the Church History. “About
ten o’clock in the morning (he says) my dear
Vilate came to see me with Sister Sarah Ann
Kingsbury.	*	*	*	They left	in one
hour, and my daughter came, and Sarah Ann
with her, and they took lunch with us at two
o’clock.”
	This visit at Brother Pierce’s I remember
very well. Several of the Apostles were pres-
ent that day, and we spent a very agreable
afternoon in conversation, while some were
engaged reading history, and we had some
good singing. Brother Taylor sang “The Up-
per Call,” composed by himself. We left at
dusk, after which father wrote in his journal:
Oh Lord, bless my dear wife and children, that
they may all be righteous from the oldest to
the youngest, and all live till they become old.”
	He came home and staid that night, but was
called up at three in the morning and went to
the trustee’s office, where he remained through
the day.
	“In the evening (he says) I rode down with
Bishop Whitney to administer to his wife and
two children, and Julia Durphy, who was work-
ing for them—they were very sick.”  A few
days after this my mother was taken sick with
chills and fever, and father could only come to
see her at night. It was three weeks before
they could meet with the Saints, and this
course they were compelled to take much of
the time, while they were builling and perlorm-
ing the work In the House of the Lord.
	Our persecuters and the murderers of the
Prophet and Patriarch supposed when they
were killed, that this act would be the death
blow to,Mormonism, but when finding out their
mistake, and that the Saints did not resent it as
they expected them to, they commenced the
same course towards the Apostles, to whom
Joseph had delivered the Keys of the Kingdom.
But knowing themselves to be innocent, and
that they would be treated in a similar manner,
having proven how much justice there was to
be found in the United States for “Mormons;”
they had no notion of putting themselves into
the power of blood-thirsty men to be placed in
chains and dungeons, for this would have
brought a dead standstill to the work upon the
Temple.
	Had not this people (as a rule) been the
most honest and sincere followers of Christ,
and willing to he led and controled in the Holy
order of the priesthood, and governed by prin-
ciples which were continually being taught and
impressed upon their minds by wiser heads,
more (I believe) would have been driven to
desperation, and long ago have attempted to


deal out retribution in their own way, as did
John D. Lee and his gang. But they were not
walking in obedience, and were entirely void
of the Holy spirit, or they could never have
committed so cruel and cowardly an act—tak-
ing advantage of that time and opportunity to
wreak vengence upon a small company of emi-
grants, although the latter were heard cursing
and threatening as they went through the
settlements, and boasting of what they had
done to the "Mormons" in Missouri, and what
they would do before they left the territory,
still they were not excusable, for they had been
taught better from the beginning, and that they
would never be justified in taking up arms or
to shed blood, except in self defence, but were
to leave all things to Him, who says, "Vengence
is mine, I will repay." They forgot this, just
as the majority of the world do, and allowedtheir
wicked passions to get the upper hand of them,
and united with the wild Indians, who, it is
well known at that time, were extreemly savage
and hostile towards the whites, and had already
caused a great amount of trouble and expense
to this people, who, it should be remembered,
were not allowed to live anywhere else within
our country's domains, but among the mur-
derous and treacherous savages, and we are not
indebted for our own lives to any effort or
assistance rendered us by Uncle Sam. But
these things are never taken into consideration
by those who cry treason and harp upon the
worn out tales of crimes which were never com-
mitted nor sanctioned by the Latter-day Saints,
for if they had it would have sealed their eter-
nal damnation, and for such we know there is
no redemption. At the same time our over
righteous accusers would rejoice could they
but get the power and opportunity to wipe us
out of existence, and they are the ones who
boast so loudly of their own spotless purlty, of
their superior wisdom, and of their great and
enlghtened Christianity. But thank God that
we are separated from such, and that we have
higher and more exalted aims than persecuting,
fighting and shedding the blood of our fellow
beings, because they will not believe nor be
governed by the "same religious" views that we
are, and that He has brought us out from
among them, and helped us to colonize this
desert land, and redeem it from its barrenness,
making it to  blossom as the rose, and that the
honest in heart are being gathered here from
every nation, kindred, tongue and people, as
Jesus declared they should be, through the in-
strumentallity of His servants, who have been
obedient to his command to go forth into the
world without purse or scrip etc., and that we
can stand up boldly and proclaim to the world
our innocence, and that we have kept ourselves
freer from crime and corruption than have any
other sect or society in the known world. Why,
we ask, in the name of conscience should not
the priests and leaders of other denominations
be made answerable for all the lawlessness and 
crimnal acts that are committed by church
members, and be brought before courts and im-
prisoned for the same, as they have sought con-
tinually to do by the authorities of the Church
of Latter-day Saints? This is something that
few stop to reflect upon, and a question which
fewer, I think, would be willing to answer; but
we do not envy them, for when we are brought 
face to face in the presence of Zion’s King,
they will then learn to their own dismay that 
in persecuting us they have been warring
against their Great and only Redeemer.
		To be Continued.

Woman as an Inventor, by Matilda Joslyn
Gage, published in the North American Review,
is a very ably written article. Mrs. Gage is one
of the most powerful speakers and intelligent
advocated of suffrage for women in the United

R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. AND PRIMARY
REPORTS.

	RICHFIELD.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Report of R. S. conference of Sevier Stake
of Zion, held at Richfield, March 17th, 1883,
at ten a.m., Sister Elizabeth Bean presiding.
Singing. Prayer by A. K. Thurber. Singing.
Minutes and report of previous conference read
and accepted. Sister Bean made a few open-
ing remarks pertaining to our religion etc.
Seven branch Societies were reported as doing
well, being in a prosperous condition consider-
ing their circumstances. Sister T. Thurber
spoke of her visit among relatives and of doing
her work in the Temple, of the heavenly spirit
therein; bore a faithful testimony to the truth
of the Latter-day work. Sister Tyler, of Beaver, 
who is helping her husband in canvassing for
the History of the Mormon Battalion spoke;
mentioned that this, the 17th of March, was
the aniversary of the first organization of the
Relief Society, effected by Joseph Smith, in
Nauvoo, Ill. and said there was a great im-
provement manifest. Brother Thurber en-
couraged the societies, and said they were a
great help to the Bishops and Presidents; said
we must give God the praise for our progress.
Conference adjourned to two p.m. Singing.
Prayer by Bishop J. Wright.
	Afternoon session. Singing. Prayer by
President F. Spencer. Singing. Verbal re-
ports of six more Societies were given.
	Sister Bean, President Spencer, Bishop
Wright and Sister Heywood encouraged and
praised the sisters of the Relief Societies for
the good they had done; exhorted them to re-
main faithful and improve in all things. Gave
very good instruction and prayed the Lord to
bless us all. Sister Bean announced that Con-
ference would be adjourned till June 15th.
Choir sang, "Oh! my Father, Thou that dwell-
est." Benediction by Bro. Perkins.
	C. A. HUNT, Secretary
	A. L. BEAN, Assistant Secretary.
May 10th, 1883.


MAYDAY PARTY.
		PINE VALLEY, MAY 7TH, 1883.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Tuesday being the first of May the members
of the Primary Association met at the meeting
house with some of their parents, also the Bish-
op and Counselors, and spent a few hours in
singing and reciting. The President, S. M. 
Burgess, her first Counselor and the committee
made the affair a very pleasant one. The Presi-
dent called upon Bishop  F. W. Jones to
make a few remarks, to which he responded,
heartily expressing himself as well pleased
with the entertainment and also thought the
Primary Association was a great benefit in the
training and entertaining of the young. He
was followed by his second Counselor Wil-
liam Gardner, who spoke very encouragingly
to the children. The party then partook of a 
bounteous repast and all tendered their heart-
felt thanks to those who had labored so assidu-
ously to make their affair a pleasant one. Danc-
ing then commenced for the children, closing
at seven o'clock.	BENNILLA E. GARDNER,
				Secretary.


THE RIGHI.
				ZURICH, Oct. 15th, 1882.
	A few hours rest, a few good-byes to the
pleasant friends we had met in Lucerne, and
we descended from our eyrie at Pension Suter,
"lingering and loth to depart."
	We had been waiting for the right day for
the Righi. Pilatus gave his assent-rather

[Column 2]
doubtfully, to be sure-but he had not given
any party a "good send off" for weeks. The
sun was shining, the air delicious, and we made
the most of his august permission-made our
last trip on Lake Lucerne, wondering anew at
its beauty.
	The Helvetia lands us at Vitznan. We ex-
amine our surroundings with no little interest
as we step on shore. We find the Righi to be
a group of mountains instead of a single one,
though to the traveler the name will generally
attach itself to the northern peak only. It was-
at first known as the "Righi Weid," or the 
pasture of the Righi. A little chapel of "Our
Lady" was erected here early in the seven-
teenth century for the use of the herdsman.
After that it was frequented by pilgrims. In
our own times it has grown greatly in favor
with tourists, and in 1873 the cog-wheel rail-
road to the Kulm was finished. The cars
themselves are not very unlike our open city
cars, except that each entrance is made more
safe by a low door. The engine is always be-
low the car, on either its up or down trip. We
examined its construction of train and road
and then committed ourselves to them without
trepidation. We were slowly lifted upward.
	The lake gradually sank below us as we
wound our singular way toward the clouds.
The higher we went the broader our view.
One mountain after another came in sight.
The dark old Burgenstock was the first to
make its appearance, rising from the opposite
bank; After that the Stauserhorn and Pilatus.
We cast many a parting look at Lucerne, away
in the distance, as we took this upward journey.
The Alps of Uri soon drew our attention.
Engleberg and the Bernese Oberland showed
us their glory as we went on. Then the
clouds wrapped themselves around us, lest we
were unable to endure so much at once.
	When we reached the highest station the fog
was so dense we were unable to see ten feet be-
yond us. Only a short distance below, the
views had been perfect. We knew the sun
was shining on the friends we had left in Lu-
cerne. Here on this mountain top we were cold
and damp-isolated from the world, it would
seem. We were not in the least disheartened.
We had watched this same mountain too often
to lose faith in it so soon. More than once
we had seen it put on a silver cap for afternoon
wear and afterward stand uncovered at the
setting of the sun.
	We let the train go down without us, and
groped around in the mist, and found on this
mountain peak friends whom we had not seen
in many months. We would be forearmed for
any possible change in the weather, so ordered
an early, simple meal. Delicious tea and
juicy, tender chops were served us here in the
clouds as daintily as we desired.
	The ascent had been made in less time than
we had expected and had not been as impres-
sive as that of Mt. Washington, which we had
accomplished some years before. We have
been told this railway has the steeper grade.
We doubt it, but we have no data by us to
verify our impression. We have here no such
sterile regions to pass over as we find in the
White Mountains. The softer features of this
landscape may take from the apparent height
of the different reaches of the grade. At all
events we are not so overawed as we were in
climbing our own giant.
	Many of those who came up when we did,
went back disheartened; our own faith had its
full reward. As the hour neared for the going
down of the sun we gathered our wraps about
us, and walked up through the damp, impene-
trable mists to the summit, only a short climb
above us. It seemed a forlorn hope that stimul-
lated us to make the effort, but the light did
appear to be stronger. How anxiously we
counted the minutes. Every few seconds one
or the other would say, "The clouds are break-


ing up." Then as we looked, it grew darker.
At last the sun! It was our supreme moment—
a moment in which hours seemed condensed.
Then the glory was withdrawn. Our eyes were
mortal. The clouds wrapped themselves close
about us; our senses grew acute, and we waited
as Moses might have waited at Pisgah. In-
visible hands parted the great curtains hung
round about us, and below us lay the promised
land; not all at once; a view here, which our
eyes drank in all eagerly, as the huge clouds
shut it out of sight again; then as this faded
out, our eyes would be drawn to a new vision
of beauty as the mists rolled themselves away
in some other direction. No words can tell the
rapid changes of that strange panorama, as we
stood and waited, spell-bound, for its unveiling.
Mountain and stream and valley lay like jewels
far below as it was given us to see them. Some-
times our eyes could follow the precipitous
sides of the mountain down to its very base,
where nestles Lake Lucerne—many-armed Lu-
cerne, with its natural battlements enfolding it
so securely, fastened our attention as the mists
were swept aside and we might look down into
its far-away depths. The almond trees were,
no doubt, blooming below, but they had dwin-
dled to tiny plants. So we watched and waited
for each coming picture. At last the clouds
settled, a shining mass not far below us, and the
silvery screen shut out the lower world. For
once we were above the earth, earthy, on this
mount of transfiguration. The clouds were
rolling beneath our feet, while the very heavens
seemed opening before us. As we looked, the
sunlight broke out clear and strong over the
snowy tops of the higher Alps, which were
gradually coming into sight. The Sentis in the
east was the first of its chain to rivet our atten-
tion. The Blaskenstock and the Uri Rothstock
exposed their glaciers; the Bernese Alps stood
up in their grandeur. We turned a longing
glance towards the Jungfrau and Pilatus. Then
the mountains were all swept away from us
again. Like Noah and the little group about
him we only seemed left of all God's children.
The sun was going down. No words can tell
what passed before our eyes in the minutes
which followed. The mists gathered in never
ending changes, to break up and roll back as
they advanced upon the sun, gyrating in un-
ceasing motion, of all imgained hues.

	And beneath us an ocean of mist, where our eye
	No longer the dwelling of man can espy,
	Through the parting clouds only the earth can be
		seen,
	Far down 'neath the vapor the meadows so green.

	All that was natural and familiar was at last
obscured. The changing lights and shades
gathered again and again, then broke and scat-
tered into fragments, as the greater glory of
the sun broke through their lesser light. "The 
heavens had declared the glory of God." As
the time passed on, great streaks of gleaming
gold and purple gathered in the western sky,
till at last the whole seemed one sea of gor-
geous light. Then the shadows darkened, and 
the vision faded.	RUTH RANDOM.
—Ex.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


THE MORAL VICTORY.

	We have received from ladies interested in
woman's elevation and emancipation in Lon-
don, with whom we exchange papers, several
pamphlets contaiaing all the particulars and
speeches in the Parliament on the repeal of
the Contagious Diseases' Acts. The vote in
the English Parliament to abolish the compul-
sory examination of women under said act, on
April 20th ult., is considered by those ac-
quainted with the subject to be a great moral
victory. It was gained by the able general-
ship of Mr. Stansfield, inspired and supported


by the steady, indomitable work of women.
A steadily increasing number of intelligent
women have been and are still laboring to bet-
ter the condition of their sex, and this is one
of the victories achieved.


DORE'S LAST WORK—The greatest works of
M. Dore's of which there has been any mention
are "Ecce Homo" and "The Ascension," two
immense pictures added to his gallery of levia-
thans; illustrations to Shakespeare, and a pic-
ture called "The Tortured Soul." This was
apparently his very last canvas, and the follow-
ing detailed description of it will therefore be
of interest: The picture represents a young
monk seated before an organ in the choir of the
Gothic Church. His fingers stray over the
keys, while his attenuated countenance and
great, hollow dark eyes are turned toward the
spectator. The church below is thronged with
white-robed monks like himself, some of them
bearing lighted tapers, while the painted win-
dows overhead glow jewel-like in the gloom be-
hind the altar. At the young organist's side,
amid the sunny light that streams through the
arched windows, stands a female form. vague,
vaporous, and beautiful as white mist amid the
golden rays. It is the vision of his lost love
that haunts the youthful ascetic, the embodied
remembrance of the woman for whose sake he
sought refuge in a cloister.
